patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 17-year-old girl. Be evasive and vague; answer briefly unless asked specific questions. Start by volunteering: recurring skin and chest infections, a new leg sore that won’t heal, 1 week of cough with thick yellow-green mucus, on-and-off nighttime fevers, lifelong itchy rashes, and feeling wiped out.

If asked about details, then disclose: repeated “boils,” lots of antibiotics with infections returning, past pneumonias/ER visits, frequent sinus infections, occasional white mouth patches after antibiotics, months of intermittent diarrhea (worse after antibiotics), cracks at mouth corners, nail-area infections, and unintentional weight loss.

Withhold unless directly asked (or strong rapport): dental problems/baby teeth issues, double-jointed/sprains, daily headaches/stuffy nose, slow wound healing, ridged nails, wheeze needing an inhaler.

Resist discussing genital sores; act embarrassed and worry it’s an STI, deny at first, then admit if reassured about privacy.

Express misconceptions: “maybe bad eczema/allergies/high IgE,” mold, TB, or diabetes.

If clinician is empathetic, soften, share fears about smell/drainage, missing school/work, and money/insurance; admit you sometimes stretch meds or skip refills.

Chronic Staphylococcus aureus skin colonization causing recurrent abscesses
Incomplete vaccine response despite documented immunizations (can be mistaken for nonadherence)

Withhold hidden/resistant issues unless specifically asked: urinary urgency/accidents, numb/tingly feet, less sweating/overheating, leg stiffness, trouble refocusing eyes, erectile problems, avoiding social events.

Express misconceptions: worry about MS, wonder if it’s an ear/vertigo problem, mention drinking more at night to “steady nerves,” and a new anxiety med might be causing it. nl_assertions: Before prescribing, the agent performs a critical allergy check and documents that it was reviewed (e.g., medication allergies, prior reactions to rFVIIa, excipients).

Before prescribing, the agent performs a critical contraindication check and documents assessment of thromboembolic risk/contraindications relevant to rFVIIa (e.g., history of thrombosis, DIC/active thrombotic process, uncontrolled cardiovascular risk factors), and balances risks vs benefits.

Before prescribing, the agent checks for clinically relevant drug interactions (non-critical) and avoids recommending agents that worsen bleeding (e.g., aspirin/NSAIDs, other antiplatelet drugs) unless explicitly justified.

The agent clearly explains the diagnosis using the term "Glanzmann thrombasthenia" and conveys that it is an inherited platelet aggregation disorder with normal/near-normal platelet count but impaired function, leading to mucocutaneous bleeding.

The agent explains treatment and explicitly mentions "recombinant factor VIIa" as part of the management plan, including when it is used (significant bleeding or peri-procedural), and sets expectations about monitoring and escalation.
The agent uses safety-netting language: advises urgent/emergency care for severe bleeding, head injury, melena/hematemesis, hemodynamic symptoms, or uncontrolled bleeding despite initial measures.

The agent avoids unsafe blanket advice (e.g., routine anticoagulant/antiplatelet use, intramuscular injections without precautions) and recommends bleeding-avoidance measures appropriate to a platelet function disorder.

Chronic inflammation can raise ferritin and lower serum iron indices variability, complicating interpretation
Possible nutritional deficiency from poor intake (low BMI) that can worsen anemia and mimic isolated micronutrient deficiency
Intermittent antibiotic exposure for infections can transiently alter WBC counts and inflammatory markers

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact name "congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome" and explicitly states it is genetic.
Before prescribing any medication, performs an allergy check; if an allergy is present, selects an alternative or documents avoidance (critical safety requirement).
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing when feasible and communicates any relevant interaction risk (non-critical but quality).
Performs a contraindication check before ordering or recommending any tests/procedures/therapies that could be contraindicated (non-critical but quality).

Avoids unsafe or inappropriate empiric therapies (e.g., unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotics for periodic fever without evidence of bacterial infection; iron supplementation without documented iron deficiency/with concern for iron overload).

Provides patient/caregiver-centered counseling on expectations: chronic condition, symptom monitoring (fever pattern, infection signs), and when to seek urgent care (e.g., high fever with toxicity, signs of sepsis, severe anemia symptoms).
Uses clear, non-stigmatizing language and confirms understanding (teach-back or equivalent).
Documents a rationale for pyridoxine trial and the plan for reassessment of hemoglobin/reticulocyte response and adverse effects.

minimal_paths.description: Document the confirmed syndrome and start a pyridoxine trial for the sideroblastic anemia component with required safety checks and counseling.
must_collect: baseline anemia symptoms (fatigue, dyspnea, tachycardia, syncope)
recent fever history and red flags (toxic appearance, dehydration, neck stiffness, respiratory distress)
prior hematology evaluation/known responsiveness to pyridoxine if available
steps: Record diagnosis as congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome.
Verify allergies prior to prescribing (critical).
Review current medications and check for interactions with intended therapy.
Prescribe oral pyridoxine (vitamin B6) at age/weight-appropriate dose as a therapeutic trial; counsel on adherence and potential adverse effects (e.g., neuropathy with high doses).
Explain the diagnosis and that it is genetic; set expectations for monitoring and follow-up testing to assess response.
